one_line_takeaway: A non-adversarially-trained CLIP variant already provides meaningful 'proxy' robustness against adversarial examples crafted on a different CLIP architecture, and this can be distilled into a target CLIP via a two-phase learning-rate-decoupled training recipe (HPT-GPD) that raises zero-shot adversarial accuracy (35.16% vs PMG's 29.95% under PGD-10, avg over 16 datasets) while also improving clean accuracy, without requiring an adversarially-trained teacher.
key_finding: Under PGD-10 at 1/255 (Table 1), HPT-GPD reaches 35.16% average adversarial accuracy vs 29.95% for PMG (prior SOTA) and 26.96% for FT-TeCoA, an improvement of 5.21 points in average adversarial accuracy over PMG as stated by the authors, while simultaneously achieving higher average clean accuracy (57.75%) than PMG (55.18%) and even slightly higher than FT-Standard fine-tuning on clean data alone (54.76%). Under the stronger AutoAttack (Table 2), HPT-GPD reaches 11.43% average robust accuracy vs 5.58% for PMG and 1.74% for vanilla CLIP. The ablation (Table 3) shows L_RT-CLIP alone raises adversarial accuracy to 38.06% but drops clean accuracy to 52.31% (a 2.87-point drop vs the L_RT-CLIP+L_GA combination's 57.75%), and adding L_GA restores clean accuracy at some adversarial-accuracy cost, netting a higher combined average (46.46% vs 45.19% vs 42.57% baseline). The method also survives an adaptive attack that jointly perturbs target and proxy gradients (Table 4: 42.48% avg adversarial accuracy vs 18.07% for a non-adversarial ensemble and 11.72% for clean-sample-only distillation), and the phenomenon generalizes beyond CLIP to ALIGN (Table 7) and to CLIP-RN50 target pairs (Fig. 8), but the authors explicitly show (Table 8) that the same proxy-robustness phenomenon does NOT hold for traditional single-modality classifiers (ResNet/VGG/MobileNet/ShuffleNet/RepVGG on CIFAR-scale models), where cross-model transfer of adversarial examples remains effective (i.e., no proxy robustness), leading them to conjecture the effect is specific to VLMs' joint image-text contrastive pretraining rather than to architectural heterogeneity per se.
core_contribution: The paper (1) identifies and names 'proxy adversarial robustness': a vanilla (non-adversarially-trained) CLIP model retains most of its accuracy against adversarial examples that were crafted against a different, heterogeneous CLIP architecture (e.g. examples crafted on ViT-B/16 largely fail to transfer to ViT-B/32, RN50, RN101), and (2) proposes Heterogeneous Proxy Transfer with Generalization-Pivot Decoupling (HPT-GPD), a training recipe that distills this proxy robustness from a frozen, never-adversarially-trained proxy CLIP into a target CLIP without requiring an expensive adversarially-trained teacher. HPT-GPD splits adversarial fine-tuning into two phases with different learning rates: a low-LR 'generalization-anchored warm-up' that aligns the target's predictions on adversarial samples with the frozen proxy's predictions on clean samples (Eq. 8, L_GA) while an EMA of the target's own weights is maintained, followed by a high-LR 'generalization-pulled HPT' phase that performs the actual robust-transfer KL distillation (Eq. 5, L_RT-CLIP) while periodically pulling the weights back toward the EMA checkpoint from phase one (Eq. 10) to prevent catastrophic loss of natural generalization.
threat_model: Standard L_infinity-bounded adversarial perturbation of input images at test time against CLIP-style zero-shot image classifiers, with the goal of causing misclassification. Perturbation budgets used are 1/255 (main experiments), and 4/255 and 8/255 (Table 5/Fig. 7, 'large attack budgets' ablation). Attacks evaluated: PGD-10 (main results, Table 1-2 training/eval), AutoAttack (standard version, APGD-CE + APGD-DLR, Table 2), and an adaptive white-box attack in which the perturbation is generated using gradients from both the target and the proxy model jointly (Table 4, 'Adaptive Attack' row) to test whether the defense is merely obfuscating gradients against a proxy-unaware attacker. The defender's goal is zero-shot robust + clean accuracy transferred to a target CLIP without adversarially training a large teacher from scratch.
limitations: The paper's own Sec. 5.8 states explicitly that they do not have a mechanistic explanation for why proxy adversarial robustness exists in VLMs; they only rule out 'architectural difference' as sufficient (since it fails for classifiers) and offer an untested conjecture about contrastive dual-encoder pretraining. All main results use a single perturbation budget (1/255) and a single downstream fine-tuning dataset (TinyImageNet) for the primary comparison table; larger budgets (4/255, 8/255) are tested only against TeCoA, not against PMG. All experiments use one GPU (RTX 3090) and the method is only benchmarked against two prior methods (FT-TeCoA, PMG), which the authors themselves note is because the zero-shot adversarial robustness literature for VLMs is still small. Absolute adversarial accuracies remain low in absolute terms even for the proposed method (e.g. 35.16% average under PGD-10, 11.43% under AutoAttack, single digits on some individual datasets like FGVC and EuroSAT), meaning the defense meaningfully raises the floor over baselines but does not come close to solving zero-shot adversarial robustness. The adaptive-attack evaluation (Table 4) is a reasonable but singular adaptive-attack design (joint-gradient PGD); it does not exhaustively probe the specific mechanism claimed (obfuscated gradients, gradient masking via the proxy) with a broader suite of adaptive strategies.

quantitative_results: Table 1, PGD-10 @1/255, average over 16 datasets: HPT-GPD adversarial accuracy 35.16% vs PMG 29.95% vs FT-TeCoA 26.96% vs FT-Standard 13.79% vs vanilla CLIP 17.38%
Table 1, clean accuracy average: HPT-GPD 57.75% vs PMG 55.18% vs FT-TeCoA 46.99% vs FT-Standard 54.76% vs vanilla CLIP 59.88%
Table 1, per-epoch training time: HPT-GPD 1486s vs PMG 1817s (331s faster than PMG) vs FT-TeCoA 1112s vs FT-Standard 406s
Table 2, AutoAttack average robust accuracy: HPT-GPD 11.43% vs PMG 5.58% vs vanilla CLIP 1.74%

Table 3 ablation, average of adv+clean accuracy: full method (L_RT-CLIP+L_GA) 46.46% vs L_RT-CLIP only 45.19% vs neither (baseline) 42.57%; adding L_RT-CLIP alone raises adv acc to 38.06% but drops clean acc to 52.31%, a 2.87-point clean-accuracy reduction versus the full method's 57.75%
Table 4 adaptive attack (joint target+proxy gradient PGD), average adversarial accuracy: 42.48% for HPT-GPD vs 18.07% for non-adversarial Ensemble vs 11.72% for Clean-sample-only Distillation
Table 5, large attack budgets: at 4/255, TeCoA-4/255+ours reaches 17.76% avg adv acc vs TeCoA-4/255 alone 7.89%; at 8/255, TeCoA-8/255+ours reaches 5.73% vs TeCoA-8/255 alone 1.35%

Fig.1/Sec.1 qualitative claim: ViT-B/32-based CLIP as proxy retains performance degradation of no more than 20% across multiple downstream datasets against adversarial samples from other CLIP variants
Sec.1 motivating example: naive distillation transfer from ViT-B/16 proxy to ViT-B/32 target causes natural accuracy on Food101 to drop from 75% to 61%

Table 8: proxy-robustness phenomenon does not hold for traditional classifiers -- e.g. RN20-generated adversarial examples reduce VGG16 accuracy from 92.97% (clean) to 14.57%, showing conventional cross-architecture transfer succeeds (no proxy robustness) for non-VLM classifiers
baselines_compared: PMG (Pre-trained Model Guided fine-tuning) [Wang et al., CVPR 2024]
